title: Method of Preparation for Both Aldehydes and Ketones

concepts.title: Oxidative Cleavage of Diols
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidative Cleavage of Diols Reaction

Reaction type: Oxidation-reduction

concepts.title: Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
• Aldehydes and ketones are first oxidation products of alcohols and carry carbonyl group ($>\mathbf{C}=\text{O}$) in their molecules.
  • In aldehydes group must also carry one $\text{H}$ atom linked to $\text{C}$ while in ketones no $\text{H}$ atom should be linked to $\text{C}$ atom of $\text{CO}$ group. Thus functional group of aldehydes is $-\text{CHO}$while that for ketones is
  • In aliphatic aldehydes, - CHO group is linked to alkyl (Group R - CHO) while in aromatic aldehyde, - $\text{CHO}$ group is linked to aryl group ( $\text{Ar}-\text{CHO}$ ).
  • In aliphatic ketones, the CO group is linked to alkyl groups (RCOR) while in aromatic ketones, the CO group is linked to aryl groups (ArCOAr).
  • In simple ketones, the alkyl or aryl groups linked to $\text{CO}$ group are same but in mixed ketones, the alkyl or aryl groups linked to CO group are different.
  • Aldehydes and ketones are functional isomers of each other and can be represented by the formula $\text{Cn}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{nO}$.

concepts.title: Nomenclature of aldehydes and ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
Nomenclature

  • Common names of aldehyde is derived from the corresponding carboxylic acids by replacing the suffix ic acid with aldehyde.
  • Common name of ketones is written by using the names of the alkyl groups followed by the word ketone.
  • IUPAC name of aldehydes is alkanals while that for ketones is alkanones.
  • Aldehydes are isomeric with ketones, unsaturated ethers and unsaturated alcohols, cyclic ethers as well as cyclic alcohols.

concepts.title: From Alcohols By Controlled Oxidation using K2CR2O7/H2SO4
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation of Aldehydes & Ketones

From Alcohols 

By Controlled oxidation using 

${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}/{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\text{ or KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}/{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

(i) aldehydes from 1 alcohols

$\text{RC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}\xrightarrow{\left[ O \right]}\text{RCHO}\xrightarrow{\left[ O \right]}\text{RCOOH}$

(ii) ketones from 2 alcohols

${{\text{R}}_{2}}\text{CHOH}\xrightarrow{\left[ O \right]}{{\text{R}}_{2}}\text{CO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

  • Primary alcohols can be oxidised to aldehydes (or further to carboxylic acids).
  • In aqueous media, the carboxylic acid is usually the major product.
  • PCC or PDC, which are used in dichloromethane, allow the oxidation to be stopped at the aldehyde.

 

  • Secondary alcohols can be oxidised to ketones, no further;

 

  • Tertiary alcohols cannot be oxidized.

 

Oppenauer Oxidation: 

Using a specific oxidising agent like Aluminium tertiary Butoxide $\{{{[{{(C{{H}_{3}})}_{3}}C-O]}_{3}}Al\}$ in presence of Acetone, secondary alcohols can be oxidised to ketones.

concepts.title: Cr Oxidation of Alcohols
concepts.conceptBody:
Cr oxidation of alcohols. 

Pyridinium dichromate (PDC) or Pyridinium Chlorochromate (PCC) in anhydrous media such as $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes and secondary alcohols to ketones.

concepts.title: From Acid Chlorides
concepts.conceptBody:
From Acid Chlorides (RCOCl): 

(a) For aldehydes

By reduction with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ in the presence of $\text{Pd}/\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ in Xylene.

$\text{RCOCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[Xylene]{Pd/Ba\,S{{O}_{4}}}\text{RCHO}+\text{HCl}$

  • This reaction is called Rosamund’s Reaction.
  • $\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ poisons the catalyst and reduce its efficiency. Thus, it checks the further reduction of aldehydes to alcohols.
  • If$\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ is used as a reducing agent, the produced is an alcohol

$\text{R}-\text{COCl or Ar}-\text{COCl}\xrightarrow{LiAl{{H}_{4}}}\text{RC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH or ArC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$

Note: Formaldehyde cannot be prepared by this method because formyl chloride is highly unstable and non-existing.

(b) For ketones: Acid chlorides on reaction with lithium organocuprates,${{\text{R}}_{2}}\text{CuLi}$ or $\text{A}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\text{CuLi}$ yield ketones. Here, the $\text{R}$ part of organocopper compound acts as nucleophile and displaces $\text{Cl}$ of acid chloride to undergo nucleophilic substitution.

\[R'X\,\,or\,\,ArX\xrightarrow[-LiX]{2Li}R'Li\] or \[ArLi\xrightarrow[-LiX]{CuX}R_{2}^{'}CuLi\] or \[A{{r}_{2}}CuLi\]

$\text{R}_{2}^{'}\text{CuLi}+2\text{RCOCl}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{RCOR}+\text{CuCl}+\text{Li}$

  • Grignard reagent can also react with acid Chlorides, but the product is tertiary alcohols because the Ketone produced reacts with additional$\text{RMgX}$. This shows that organo copper reagents are less reactive than Grignard reagents towards the carbonyl group of ketones and the reaction stops at the ketone formation stage.

This low reactivity shows that the organo copper compounds donot react with the functional groups with which organo-magnesium and organo-lithium reagents react. Thus, the presence of some functional groups (like$-\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}},-\text{CN},-\text{C}{{\text{O}}^{-}},-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{R}$etc.) does not interfere with the synthesis of ketone.

Example:

concepts.title: From Calcium Salt of Carboxylic Acid
concepts.conceptBody:
From Calcium Salt Of Carboxylic Acid (PYROLYSIS)

Formal dehyde is formed when calcium salt of formic acid is dry distilled.

${{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}\text{Ca}\xrightarrow{dry\,distillation}\text{HCHO}+\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

Ketones are formed when calcium salt of monobasic acid (other than formic acid) is heated.

Illustration 

 

  • Instead of using calcium salt of an acid vapours of an acid or mixture of acids can be passed over heated$\text{MnO}$ at $300\text{C}$.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+\text{HCOOH}\xrightarrow[300\,C]{MnO}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\xrightarrow[300C]{MnO}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}\xrightarrow[300\,C]{MnO}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

  • From acetoacetic ester

concepts.title: From Alkenes
concepts.conceptBody:
From alkenes

(i) By reductive ozonolysis 

Aldehydes can be prepared by the ozonolysis of alkenes of the type$\text{R}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{R}$in presence of Zn (Reductive Ozonolysis)

Ketone can also be prepared by the ozonolysis of alkenes of the type ${{\text{R}}_{2}}\text{C}=\text{C}{{\text{R}}_{2}}$

(ii) Oxo process : It involves the conversion of alkenes to aldehydes with one$\text{C}$ atom more than parent alkene.

$\text{R}-\text{CH}=\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\underset{[\text{Watergas}\}}{\mathop{\text{CO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}\,\xrightarrow{CoH{{\left( CO \right)}_{4}}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{CHO}$

(iii) Wacker’s Process 

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\text{PdC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{CuC{{l}_{2}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}+\text{Pd}+2\text{HCl}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{CH}=\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\text{PdC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{CuC{{l}_{2}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{Pd}+2\text{HCl}$

concepts.title: From Geminal Dihalides
concepts.conceptBody:
From geminal dihalides:

By alkaline hydrolysis, the geminal halides gets converted into aldehydes and ketones.

Illustration:

concepts.title: From Alkynes
concepts.conceptBody:
From Alkynes

(1) Hydration: 

Hydration of alkynes gives ketones (except$\text{CH}\equiv \text{CH}$ that gives $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}$ )

$\text{CH}\equiv \text{CH}\underset{{}}{\mathop{\xrightarrow[HgS{{O}_{4}}]{{{H}_{2}}O/{{H}^{+}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}}}\,$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{CH}\xrightarrow[HgS{{O}_{4}}]{{{H}_{2}}O/{{H}^{+}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

(2) Hydroboration - oxidation 

Hydroboration of a non-terminal alkyne followed by oxidation of the intermediate yields a ketone but terminal alkyne yields aldehyde.

concepts.title: Cleavage of Diols
concepts.conceptBody:
Cleavage of Diols

By oxidative clearage of 1,2 diols (glycols) by periodic acid$\left( \text{HI}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ or by lead tetra acetate, both aldehyde and ketones may be produced.

concepts.title: Oxidation of Toluene
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation of aromatic aldehydes and ketones

1. Oxidation of toluene

(a) Using chromyl chloride $\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}]{\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}}\underset{\text{ Benzaldehyde }}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{CHO}}}\,$

This is called Etard’s Reaction 

Note: if side chain is bigger than $-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ group, then terminal $\text{C}$ is changed to $-\text{CHO}$ group. For example,

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\text{Cr}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}}\underset{Phenyl\,ethanal}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{CHO}}}\,$

(b) Oxidation using \[Cr{{O}_{3}}\]in acetic anhydride

concepts.title: Gattermann Koch Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Gattermann Koch Reaction:

This involves formylation of aromatic ring with CO.

concepts.title: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties Of Aldehydes And Ketones

Physical Properties

  • Formaldehyde is gas but other aldehydes are liquids at room temperature.
  • Lower ketones are colourless liquids while higher ketones are colourless solids.
  • Boiling points of ald. ketones are relatively higher than hydrocarbons but lower than alcohols of comparable molecular mass.
  • Boiling points of ketones are more than the corresponding isomeric aldehydes.
  • Lower aldehydes / Ketones (up to 4 carbons) are soluble in water but higher aldehydes / ketones are almost immiscible with water.

concepts.title: Chemical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.conceptBody:
Chemical Properties 

  • Aldehydes and ketones owe their highly reactive nature to the polar carbonyl group in their molecules.
  • The electron deficient carbon atom of $\text{CO}$ group readily undergo the nucleophilic attack for initiation of reaction.
  • The general mode of reaction being:

  • Crowding at the carbonyl carbon and presence of electron releasing group hinder’s the nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon and decreases the reactivity of the compound. For example, crowding as well as electron releasing effect increases from formaldehyde to other aldehydes to ketones. Hence, reactivity decreases as follows.

  • The presence of electron withdrawing groups at the carbonyl carbon increases the reactivity.
  • The attachment of benzene ring to $\text{CO}$ group decreases the reactivity because $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond comes in conjugation with aromatic ring and resonance stabilisation of molecule occurs.
  • \[\text{CO}\] group withdraws electron density from the ring. Hence it becomes less susceptible to the electrophilic substituion.
  • Ring substitution in benzaldehyde occurs at m-position.
